query: evidence on effect of stopping inhaled glucocorticoids on COVID-19 outcomes in asthmatic or COPD patients

retrieved_docs.title: Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease Compensatory Measures -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Many COPD patients develop acute exacerbation and are admitted to intensive care units (ICU). Many factors affect the outcome, and one of the most important factors is the acid-base disorder in COPD patients. The function and clinical significance of COPD are discussed in this review article. The natural history, diagnosis, prognosis, and treatment of COPD are discussed in another article.

retrieved_docs.title: Asthma and COPD Overlap -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Regarding anti-interleukin-5 therapies, phase 2 of a randomized study published recently with benralizumab showed a tendency towards exacerbation reduction in COPD patients with blood eosinophil concentrations of 200 cells per microL or higher. However, the outcomes of two subsequent phase 3 studies in COPD were unfavorable. [27] Regarding mepolizumab, clinically and statistically significant reductions in moderate or severe exacerbations were shown in a phase 3 study of COPD patients with blood eosinophil levels at or more than 150 cells/microL at screening or 300 cells/microL in the previous year. However, a parallel study showed negative results. [28]

retrieved_docs.title: Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Inhaled corticosteroids are often used in combination with LABAs and LAMAs to decrease inflammation. A combination of ICS and LABA has been shown to be more beneficial than either of the drugs when used alone. Physicians and patients should be aware of an increased risk of developing pneumonia when treated with an ICS. Oral glucocorticoids are not indicated for long-term use and can have multiple side effects. These should instead be reserved for the management of acute exacerbations. [16] [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Acute exacerbations of COPD can be managed in an outpatient or inpatient setting depending on the severity. The assessment of severity is discussed in the previous section. Mild cases can be treated in the outpatient setting with bronchodilators, corticosteroids, and antibiotics. For moderate and severe cases, inpatient management is indicated. Hospitalized patients often require oxygen and bronchodilator therapy in the form of a SABA with or without a SAMA. Long-acting bronchodilators are typically used when the patient becomes stable and ready for discharge. Oral corticosteroids may be used, however, intravenous corticosteroids have not shown to be more efficacious compared to oral and instead may cause increased side effects. Intravenous corticosteroids may be considered if patients have a contraindication to oral intake (e.g., aspiration risk or continuous BiPAP therapy). Antibiotics should be considered if there is suspicion for a bacterial infection. Oxygen therapy can range from a nasal cannula to mechanical ventilation depending on the severity of the exacerbation. [1] [8] [22]

retrieved_docs.title: Asthma and COPD Overlap -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Another critical pharmacological intervention for patients with ACO is biological treatment which has been shown to reduce the exacerbations and use of oral steroids among asthma patients. In one study, the response to treatment with omalizumab was evaluated between individuals with asthma-COPD overlap and asthma patients. They discovered that individuals with severe allergic asthma and overlapping COPD experienced improved asthma control and health-related quality of life after receiving omalizumab medication. [16] Therefore, patients with worsening symptoms or exacerbations despite triple treatment should be examined for signs that might indicate a response to the biological asthma medications, such as raised total serum IgE and peripheral blood eosinophilia.

retrieved_docs.title: COPD and Sleep Apnea Overlap -- Treatment / Management -- Pharmacologic Therapies
retrieved_docs.content: Treatment regimens for patients with COPD are determined by the severity of symptoms and the risk of future exacerbations. Maintenance therapy typically involves the use of inhaled bronchodilators, usually long-acting beta-agonists and muscarinic antagonists, either alone or in combination, with the potential addition of inhaled glucocorticoids. All patients with COPD should have access to a short-acting bronchodilator. Generally, the preferred choice is a short-acting beta-agonist (SABA) to avoid combining a short-acting muscarinic agent (SAMA) with a long-acting muscarinic agent. Patients treated with long-acting beta-agonists alone or who use only short-acting therapy should use a combination of SABA-SAMA for rescue therapy due to the superior bronchodilator response compared to either agent alone. Patients with COPD should also receive pneumococcal, influenza, pertussis, COVID-19, and respiratory syncytial virus vaccinations.

retrieved_docs.title: Asthma and COPD Overlap -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Regarding pharmacological interventions, inhaled corticosteroids (ICS) and bronchodilators continue to be the mainstay of treatment for most patients with ACO, much like asthma and COPD. Choosing the best bronchodilator medication for these patients is still uncertain, though. In its most recent edition, the Global Initiative for Asthma (GINA) and the Global Initiative for Obstructive lung disease  (GOLD) advise starting asthma therapy after being diagnosed with ACO. This is justified by ICS's crucial role in treating patients with asthma-like symptoms. However, formal data are scarce because ACO patients have always been excluded from clinical studies of asthma and COPD drugs. [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Asthma and COPD Overlap -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Macrolides have many applications in chronic obstructive respiratory conditions, especially with COPD patients. They have multifaceted therapeutic benefits, including antibacterial, anti-inflammatory, and immunomodulatory effects. A recent Cochrane review found inadequate evidence to support using macrolides in chronic asthma to reduce exacerbations, oral steroid usage, quality of life, or rescue medication use. However, there is relatively little data regarding their use in asthma. [31] Macrolides could be useful in ACO patients with recurrent exacerbations and non-eosinophilic characteristics, but further investigation is required before drawing firm conclusions.

retrieved_docs.title: Asthma and COPD Overlap -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: Reduce the patient's risk of infection because some infections can exacerbate ACO symptoms. By receiving specific vaccinations, patients can reduce their risk. These include receiving an annual flu shot, the pneumococcal vaccine, and subsequent doses of the COVID-19 vaccine. Avoid triggers, such as dust or pollution, that worsen a patient's breathing.

retrieved_docs.title: Inhaled Corticosteroids -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Recently updated guidelines also recommend ICS to be used for acute asthma symptoms in conjunction with beta-2 agonists in adolescents and adults. [4] Inhaled corticosteroids are also prescribed off-label (non-FDA approved) to manage chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD). Up to 40% to 50% of patients with COPD receive inhaled corticosteroid therapy. Data suggests that these medications decreased the number of exacerbations and may slow the progression of lung disease. [5] There is, however, minimal impact of inhaled corticosteroids on lung function and mortality. Inhaled corticosteroids are most often used in COPD as an adjunct to long-acting inhaled bronchodilators, but the clinician may initiate them earlier if there is an asthmatic component in a given patient's lung disease. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Asthma and COPD Overlap -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The definition used commonly for ACO is the presence of airflow obstruction in individuals with a history of asthma and older than 40. [3] According to the Global Initiative for Asthma (GINA) and GOLD, patients usually have chronic airway disease symptoms with cough, wheezing, recurrent lower respiratory tract infection, and asthma and COPD features. [2] This review will describe the clinical presentation, evaluation, and management of patients with ACO.

retrieved_docs.title: Asthma and COPD Overlap -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Moreover, as individuals with frequent exacerbations experience a higher loss of lung function, a reduced FEV1 is linked to worsening disease severity, especially concerning people with ACO. [36] According to another study's findings, persistent asthma with chronic airflow restriction has a higher mortality rate. [37]

retrieved_docs.title: Asthma and COPD Overlap -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Asthma and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) are the most common obstructive lung diseases observed during clinical practice. They have common characteristics and similar clinical presentation. Moreover, they usually have the same presentation on pulmonary function tests (PFT). Therefore, the term "asthma-COPD overlap" (ACO) was used to describe the cases in that they have both features of asthma and COPD. Gibson first described ACO in 2009. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Emphysema -- Treatment / Management -- Inhaled corticosteroid
retrieved_docs.content: Inhaled corticosteroids (ICS) are used as a step-up treatment approach to bronchodilators. ICS medications include beclomethasone, budesonide, and fluticasone. While these medications can be effective, they may cause side effects such as local infection, cough, and pneumonia. Oral systemic corticosteroids are typically used for all patients experiencing COPD exacerbation. However, they are generally avoided in stable patients due to the increased risk of adverse effects.

retrieved_docs.title: Asthma and COPD Overlap -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: For as-needed symptom alleviation, all ACO patients should have prompt access to an inhaled bronchodilator with a quick start of action (such as a short-acting beta agonist, short-acting muscarinic antagonist, or combination). Also, avoiding treatment with Long-Acting Beta-Agonists (LABA) alone (LABA monotherapy) without ICS in patients with asthma symptoms is a crucial component of ACO management. [21] Additionally, mounting data points to a connection between higher blood eosinophil levels and ICS responsiveness in COPD. [22] [23]

retrieved_docs.title: Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease Compensatory Measures -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: According to epidemiological data, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) is expected to be the third leading cause of death. [1] Although the prognosis of these patients is improving with new treatment modalities, the mortality in these patients is still high. [2] [3]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 4 -- Community 2909
retrieved_docs.content: Glucocorticoid inhalants for obstructive airway disease comprise several specific agents, including prednison, prednisolone acetate, prednisolone sodium succinate, and triamcinolone diacetate. These inhaled glucocorticoids are employed to reduce airway inflammation in conditions such as asthma and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, and each listed medication represents a distinct formulation or derivative within the broader class, offering clinicians a range of options tailored to pharmacologic properties and patient‑specific considerations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 2 -- Community 700
retrieved_docs.content: The class “Adrenergics inhalants in combination with corticosteroids or other drugs, excl. anticholinergics” comprises several specific inhaled combination products that pair a β‑adrenergic bronchodilator with an inhaled corticosteroid to provide both bronchodilation and anti‑inflammatory therapy for obstructive airway diseases. Included in this class are albuterol + beclomethasone drug combinations, budesonide / formoterol, formoterol / mometasone, and vilanterol + fluticasone furoate. Each of these formulations is a member of the adrenergic‑corticosteroid combination category, distinguishing them from inhaled therapies that contain anticholinergic agents. This grouping highlights their shared pharmacologic strategy of combining a rapid‑acting or long‑acting β‑agonist with a corticosteroid, which is central to modern asthma and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 2 -- Community 592
retrieved_docs.content: Glucocorticoid inhalants for obstructive airway disease constitute a therapeutic class that is classified under the ATC category “OTHER DRUGS FOR OBSTRUCTIVE AIRWAY DISEASES, INHALANTS.” This class includes several specific glucocorticoid formulations used as inhaled steroids, namely dexamethasone sodium phosphate, prednisolone sodium succinate, and triamcinolone diacetate. These inhaled glucocorticoids are employed in the management of obstructive airway conditions such as asthma and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, providing anti‑inflammatory effects to improve airway function and reduce exacerbations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 1 -- Community 101
retrieved_docs.content: adrenergic and anticholinergic inhalants, corticosteroid combinations, antiallergic agents, systemic bronchodilators, and biologic monoclonal antibodies—provide a structured framework that supports clinicians in selecting appropriate pharmacologic strategies for patients with obstructive airway diseases and related inflammatory conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 1209
retrieved_docs.content: Adrenergics inhalants in combination with corticosteroids or other drugs, excl. anticholinergics is a therapeutic class that comprises several fixed‑dose inhaler formulations used to treat obstructive airway diseases. Within this class, the albuterol + beclomethasone drug combination, the budesonide / formoterol combination, and the formoterol / mometasone combination are all members. Each of these products pairs a rapid‑acting β2‑adrenergic agonist (albuterol or formoterol) with an inhaled corticosteroid (beclomethasone, budesonide, or mometasone) to provide both bronchodilation and anti‑inflammatory effects while deliberately excluding anticholinergic agents. This grouping highlights the shared pharmacologic strategy of combining adrenergic bronchodilators with corticosteroids to improve symptom control and reduce exacerbations in patients with asthma or chronic obstructive pulmonary disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 3 -- Community 1386
retrieved_docs.content: Glucocorticoid inhalants for obstructive airway disease constitute a class of inhaled corticosteroid medications that includes dexamethasone acetate, dexamethasone sodium phosphate, prednisolone sodium succinate, and triamcinolone diacetate. These agents are employed to diminish airway inflammation in conditions such as asthma and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, delivering anti‑inflammatory effects directly to the respiratory tract. The presence of multiple steroid salts and esters within this group provides clinicians with options to tailor therapy based on pharmacokinetic characteristics, potency, and individual patient tolerance, thereby enhancing the overall management of obstructive airway disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1234
retrieved_docs.content: Budesonide, a glucocorticoid employed to reduce airway inflammation, is one of the two active components of the fixed‑dose combination inhaler budesonide/formoterol; this medication pairs budesonide with the long‑acting β2‑agonist formoterol to deliver both anti‑inflammatory and bronchodilator effects for the management of asthma and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 1098
retrieved_docs.content: Glucocorticoid inhalants for obstructive airway disease are a therapeutic class that includes dexamethasone sodium phosphate, prednisolone acetate, prednisolone sodium succinate, and triamcinolone diacetate. These inhaled corticosteroids are employed to reduce airway inflammation in conditions such as asthma and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, thereby improving airflow, decreasing the frequency of exacerbations, and enhancing overall patient outcomes. The presence of multiple specific glucocorticoid salts and esters illustrates the range of formulations available for targeted inhalation therapy in obstructive airway disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 0 -- Community 13
retrieved_docs.content: Overall, the community’s knowledge delineates how anticholinergic and adrenergic agents are organized by selectivity, route of administration, and combination therapy, and it clarifies their specific indications and contraindications in acute inflammatory respiratory conditions such as acute bronchitis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 700
retrieved_docs.content: Tiotropium Bromide/Olodaterol is classified as a member of the therapeutic group “Adrenergics in combination with anticholinergics including triple combinations with corticosteroids.” This class comprises agents that combine bronchodilatory adrenergic activity with anticholinergic effects, and may be formulated together with corticosteroids to provide triple therapy for obstructive airway diseases such as chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD). By belonging to this combination class, Tiotropium Bromide/Olodaterol offers synergistic bronchodilation through dual mechanisms, enhancing airway patency and symptom control in patients requiring advanced inhaled therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 0 -- Community 5
retrieved_docs.content: (akutní nemoc) is a contraindication for the use of long‑acting β2‑agonists such as salmeterol and formoterol, as their pharmacologic profile is unsuitable for rapidly evolving symptoms; in such settings clinicians prefer short‑acting bronchodilators. This hierarchical organization and the noted contraindications are essential for safe prescribing and effective management of respiratory conditions such as asthma and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1236
retrieved_docs.content: Glucocorticoid inhalants for obstructive airway disease constitute a therapeutic class of inhaled corticosteroid medications employed to manage inflammatory airway conditions such as asthma and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease. Within this class, prednisolone acetate, prednisolone sodium succinate, and triamcinolone diacetate are specific glucocorticoid agents that are formulated for inhalation to reduce airway inflammation and improve respiratory function.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 0 -- Community 7
retrieved_docs.content: All these entities and relationships are encompassed within the higher‑level ATC category “Drugs for Obstructive Airway Diseases,” which groups adrenergic inhalants, inhaled corticosteroids and other inhaled respiratory therapies, providing a structured framework for their pharmacologic classification and therapeutic use.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 1 -- Community 127
retrieved_docs.content: options. Non‑steroidal antiallergic inhalants for obstructive airway diseases are also “isa” members of the same ATC inhalant group, highlighting their role as steroid‑free anti‑allergic agents. The overarching category DRUGS FOR OBSTRUCTIVE AIRWAY DISEASES is an inverse‑isa of both adrenergics, inhalants and other inhaled drug classes, thereby encompassing all inhaled adrenergic agents, inhaled corticosteroids, antiallergics, and related combination products, while being inversely related to adrenergics for systemic use to distinguish inhaled from systemic formulations. These hierarchical and membership relationships provide a comprehensive framework for selecting appropriate bronchodilator, anti‑inflammatory, and combination therapies based on route of administration, receptor selectivity, and contraindications, supporting safe and effective patient care in asthma, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, and related obstructive airway conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1238
retrieved_docs.content: The category “Adrenergics inhalants in combination with corticosteroids or other drugs, excl. anticholinergics” encompasses several specific inhaled drug pairings used for airway diseases. This class includes the “albuterol, beclomethasone drug combinations,” which pair the short‑acting β2‑agonist albuterol with the corticosteroid beclomethasone, as well as the combination product “vilanterol and fluticasone furoate,” which pairs the long‑acting β2‑agonist vilanterol with the corticosteroid fluticasone furoate. Within the latter combination, fluticasone furoate is identified as a constituent component, reflecting its role as the corticosteroid element of the vilanterol‑fluticasone furoate inhaler. Together, these relationships illustrate how various adrenergic‑corticosteroid inhaler formulations are organized under a broader therapeutic class that excludes anticholinergic agents, highlighting their shared mechanism of delivering bronchodilation and anti‑inflammatory effects for conditions such as asthma and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 2153
retrieved_docs.content: Glucocorticoid inhalants for obstructive airway disease and Glucocorticoids, Systemic represent two related categories of glucocorticoid therapy that share anti‑inflammatory mechanisms but differ in route of administration, with the former delivered by inhalation to treat airway obstruction and the latter administered systemically. Prednison (prednisone) is a specific systemic glucocorticoid medication and therefore belongs to the Glucocorticoids, Systemic class. Although classification schemes may list inhaled glucocorticoids as a subcategory within the broader glucocorticoid family, prednison is not an inhaled agent but a systemic drug. The relationship between Glucocorticoid inhalants for obstructive airway disease and Glucocorticoids, Systemic highlights their common pharmacologic basis while emphasizing the clinical distinction between inhaled and systemic delivery for optimal patient management.
